Re:
February 15 Letter to Marty Pike
Dear Natalie:
During our weekly review of Free correspondence,
I noticed your reply to Marty Pike, of Winner, Wagner
and Associates. answering questions on the legality of
a lobbyist making a Contribution to a local election,
where the spouse of the candidate is
a state official.
It would be helpful
to receive further
clarification on why the Commission staff concludes
that a political contribution is a payment that personally
benefits the candidate,
or his or her family,
who is
being supported by the committee to whom the contribution
is made.
As you know,
at the present time,
persons
required to file reports under Government Code Section
35109 report campaign contributions pursuant to sub-
paragraph
(c) and according to your letter, they will
also be required to disclose these same contributions
pursuant to subeparagraph
(c),
Without re-reading the Commission's Donald C.
Green opinion, don't I remember an argument that the law
does not presume a redundancy, which appears to exist if
a political contribution is also considered a gift to
that person?
Also,
if this is considered a Payment to
benefit,
is
it also considered an exchange and therefore
not reportable pursuant to sub
(c) but only pursuant to
sub
(db
(as explained in Rich Eichman's letter of
November
1,
1976
to Mark Windischﬁr


2

8.
1977
Page two
And, as you know,
this conclusion is contrary
to tax law that holds that
the candidate
is
not a
beneficiary of campaign contributions made to his or
her committee.
Your consideration of this question is
sincerely appreciatedr
Very t;u1y yours, /

sections.conclusion: The FPPC clarifies that campaign contributions made by a lobbyist are permissible but must be reported on Form 520 (lobbyist report). When contributions are made by a lobbyist employer, they should be reported as 'Contributions' on Part 3 of Form 650, as required by Government Code Section 86109(a). The lobbyist employer has no separate reporting obligation for contributions made by the lobbyist individually.
sections.question_synthetic: The FPPC Staff Counsel responds to clarify an earlier advice letter regarding whether a lobbyist's campaign contribution that benefits a state official's spouse constitutes a reportable payment, and whether such contributions should be reported under multiple provisions of the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Campaign contributions made by a lobbyist or lobbyist employer to influence administrative action benefiting a state official's family member are permissible and must be reported on the appropriate forms (Form 520 for lobbyists, Form 650 Part 3 for lobbyist employers) without requiring duplicate reporting elsewhere.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document consists of two letters: an incoming request from Nielsen (March 3, 1977) seeking clarification on a prior FPPC advice letter, and the FPPC's response (also dated around March 8, 1977) from Staff Counsel. The document quality is poor with significant OCR errors and formatting issues. No formal question/conclusion section headers present; content extracted from letter body text.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: The FPPC Staff Counsel responds to clarify an earlier advice letter regarding whether a lobbyist's campaign contribution that benefits a state official's spouse constitutes a reportable payment, and whether such contributions should be reported under multiple provisions of the Political Reform Act.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC clarifies that campaign contributions made by a lobbyist are permissible but must be reported on Form 520 (lobbyist report). When contributions are made by a lobbyist employer, they should be reported as 'Contributions' on Part 3 of Form 650, as required by Government Code Section 86109(a). The lobbyist employer has no separate reporting obligation for contributions made by the lobbyist individually.

It would be helpful to receive further clarification on why the Commission staff concludes that a political contribution is a payment that personally benefits the candidate, or his or her family, who is being supported by the committee to whom the contribution is made. embedding.summary: This is a follow-up correspondence from FPPC Staff Counsel responding to questions raised by Vigo Nielsen, Jr. regarding an earlier advice letter about campaign contributions made by lobbyists when the benefit accrues to a state official's family member. The Counsel clarifies reporting requirements under the Political Reform Act and addresses concerns about redundant reporting.
